title: Imperative Sentences
concept: Imperative sentences are used to give commands, instructions, requests, advice, warnings, and suggestions. The subject 'You' is implied and not written. The sentence always begins with the base form (V1) of the verb.
explanation:
Imperative sentences kisi ko kuch karne ya na karne ke liye kehte hain. Inme Subject (You) nahi likha jata — wo implied hota hai. Sentence hamesha Verb ki Base Form se shuru hota hai.

Formula:
Affirmative: Verb (V1) + Object
Negative: Don't / Never + Verb (V1)
Polite: Please / Kindly + Verb (V1)
Inclusive: Let's + Verb (V1)

Examples:
Close the door. (command)
Please sit down. (polite request)
Don't run here. (negative)
Let's start. (inclusive suggestion)
hindiExplanation:
Imperative sentences वो sentences होते हैं जिनमें हम किसी को:
• कुछ करने को कहते हैं (command): 'दरवाज़ा बंद करो।'
• विनती करते हैं (request): 'कृपया बैठ जाइए।'
• मना करते हैं (negative): 'यहाँ मत दौड़ो।'
• सुझाव देते हैं (suggestion): 'चलो शुरू करते हैं।'

इन sentences में Subject 'You' छुपा होता है — लिखा नहीं जाता।
हमेशा Verb की Base Form (V1) use होती है — closed, closing नहीं।
rules: हमेशा Verb की Base Form (V1) से शुरू करो: open, sit, go, come
Polite बनाने के लिए 'Please' start में या end में: Please sit. / Sit, please.
Formal polite के लिए 'Kindly': Kindly submit the form.
Negative के लिए 'Don't' + V1: Don't shout. Don't be late.
Absolute negative के लिए 'Never': Never lie. Never give up.
Inclusive suggestion के लिए 'Let's' + V1: Let's eat. Let's begin.
Warning के लिए 'Be careful / Watch out / Mind': Be careful! Watch your step!
Question tag जोड़ने के लिए: Close the door, will you? / Come here, won't you?
Imperative में -ing form नहीं होती: 'Please be coming' WRONG → 'Please come' CORRECT

commonMistakes.why: Imperative में 'You' subject नहीं लिखते — it is implied
'Please to' गलत है — Please के बाद directly V1
Imperative में -ing form नहीं — base form (V1) use करो
'Don't to' गलत है — Don't के बाद directly V1
'Let's' में apostrophe ज़रूरी है — Let + us = Let's
Never के बाद भी base form (V1) — -ing नहीं
Water uncountable noun है — general sense में 'the' नहीं
Imperative में हमेशा V1 (base form) — V2 नहीं

memoryTrick: VERB से शुरू = IMPERATIVE! जब sentence Verb (close, sit, go, open, don't) से start हो — वो Imperative है। 'You' देखो तो Declarative है। Verb देखो तो Imperative है!
speakingTips: रोज़ घर में 10 Imperatives बोलो — माँ को, भाई को, दोस्त को: 'Bring water. Close the window. Help me.'
Tone practice करो: Same sentence को Strict Tone और Friendly Tone में बोलो — 'Come here!' vs 'Come here, please.'
Role-play करो: Teacher, Boss, Friend — तीनों के तरीके से commands बोलो
Mirror के सामने बोलो — body language के साथ imperatives practice करो
Office scenarios practice करो: 'Please review this. Kindly revert by EOD. Send me the report.'

vocabulary.meaning: To hand in or send something officially

vocabulary.example: Please proceed to the next step.
Consider all the options before deciding.
Implement the new policy from Monday.
Please respond to the email today.
Coordinate with the team for the event.
Notify the team about the change.
Complete the form and sign it.
Update the records by end of day.
Follow the safety guidelines at all times.

officialExamples: Please read the instructions carefully before starting the exam.
Do not use mobile phones inside the examination hall.
Write your roll number clearly on the answer sheet.
Kindly attach all supporting documents with your application.
Report any suspicious activity to the nearest officer.
Do not fold or staple the answer booklet.
Sign and date the declaration form at the bottom.
Use only blue or black ink pens.
Maintain discipline at all times on the premises.
Submit the form within 30 days of the date of issue.
dailyLifeExamples: Wake up early and exercise daily.
Brush your teeth twice a day.
Don't talk with your mouth full.
Lock the door before you sleep.
Take an umbrella — it might rain.
Don't forget to charge your phone.
Switch off the fan before leaving.
Finish your homework before watching TV.
officeExamples: Please send the report by 5 PM today.
Kindly review the attached document and revert.
Attend the all-hands meeting at 11 AM sharp.
Do not share confidential data outside the organization.
Update the project tracker before end of day.
Please CC the manager on all client emails.
Schedule a meeting with the team for Thursday.
Follow up with the client regarding pending approvals.
Ensure all deliverables are met before the deadline.
Please respond to the client's email within 24 hours.
Use professional language in all written communication.
Log your work hours in the system daily.
Raise a ticket if you face any IT issues.
Coordinate with the design team for the presentation.
Submit your expense reports by the 5th of every month.
story:
Riya's First Day at Work

On Riya's first day at her new job, her manager gave her many instructions.

'Come in, Riya! Welcome to the team,' the manager said with a smile. 'Sit down. Let me show you around. First, read this employee handbook carefully. It has all the important guidelines. Don't skip any section.'

The HR executive joined them and said, 'Fill out this form and submit it today. Sign at the bottom. Attach a passport-sized photo. Don't forget to write your emergency contact number.'

The IT team came over next. 'Set up your email now. Choose a strong password. Don't share it with anyone. Install the company VPN on your laptop. If you face any issue, call extension 101.'

At lunchtime, her colleague Arjun said, 'Come, let's go to the cafeteria. Try the dal rice — it's really good! Don't miss the gulab jamun on Fridays!'

By the end of the day, Riya's manager said, 'Well done today! Always be on time. Ask questions whenever you're confused. Never hesitate to ask for help. We're a team here.'

Riya smiled and thought: English imperative sentences are everywhere — and now she understood them perfectly!
essay:
The Power of Clear Instructions

Imperative sentences are the backbone of clear, effective communication. Every day, we receive and give dozens of commands, requests, and instructions — and imperative sentences make this possible.

In daily life, imperatives help us manage our household and family. 'Wake up early. Eat your breakfast. Lock the door.' These simple instructions keep life organized. Good parents use a mix of gentle requests ('Please clean your room') and firm commands ('Don't touch the stove') to guide their children safely.

In the professional world, imperatives are essential. Managers give instructions: 'Submit the report by Friday. Attend the training session. Follow up with the client.' Clear, direct imperatives save time and avoid misunderstandings. A well-written email with precise imperatives — 'Please review the attached document and confirm receipt' — reflects professionalism.

In education, teachers use imperatives to guide students: 'Open your books. Read paragraph two. Answer the following questions.' These commands create a structured learning environment.

However, tone matters greatly. The same imperative can sound harsh or polite depending on how it is said. 'Sit down!' sounds aggressive, while 'Please have a seat' sounds welcoming. Adding 'please' or 'kindly,' softening the voice, or using 'Let's' instead of a direct command transforms the interaction.

In conclusion, mastering imperative sentences means mastering the art of communication. Whether at home, at work, or in public, knowing how to give clear, polite, and appropriate instructions is an invaluable life skill.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.text: Good morning, everyone. Please take your seats. Let's start today's meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Sure, sir. Should I close the door?

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.text: Yes, please close the door and switch off the lights near the window. Priya, please share the presentation on the screen.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Done. Shall I begin from slide one?

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.text: Yes, start from slide one. Everyone, please focus. Don't use your phones during the presentation.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.text: Good idea. Write down the action items. Don't miss anything important. Priya, continue please.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Sure. So, look at this chart. Notice the increase in sales during Q3. Compare it with Q2.

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.text: Good point. Team, analyze these numbers carefully. Think about what drove this growth. Discuss with your respective teams and submit your insights by Thursday.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Understood. Should we send it to you directly?

dialogue.speaker: Manager
dialogue.text: Yes, send me an email. CC Priya as well. Don't forget to mention your department in the subject line. Any questions? If not, let's wrap up. Thank you everyone — great work this week!
